intro: The Word of God in Scripture designates both the spoken divine address to humanity and the written record of that address — ultimately understood as one coherent testimony. Hebrews 4:12 describes the written Word as "living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and of spirit, of joints and of marrow, and discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart." 2 Timothy 3:16–17 grounds the Scripture's authority in inspiration: "All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness." John 17:17 identifies the word with sanctifying truth: "Thy word is truth." Psalm 119 is entirely devoted to celebrating the many dimensions of God's revealed word — its reliability, life-giving character, and function as a lamp and light. In the prophetic tradition, the "word of the LORD" that came to the prophets was experienced as a powerful, external address that compelled proclamation (Jeremiah 20:9; Amos 3:8). Peter affirms that no prophecy came from human will but from the Holy Spirit carrying along the human authors (2 Peter 1:21).
